content.text: Why Role-Based Curricula Rather Than Universal Training

A single GenAI training programme applied uniformly across an organisation produces two failure modes simultaneously: it overtrains people who do not use AI tools in their daily work (wasting their time and reducing engagement) and undertrains the people who do (leaving them with conceptual knowledge but no workflow-specific skill). Role-based curricula solve this by assigning different training depth, content, and assessment methods based on how each role interacts with GenAI in practice.

The goal of a role-based curriculum is not for every employee to understand AI -- it is for every employee to be able to do their job safely and effectively in an AI-augmented environment. Those are different learning objectives and require different training designs.

attrs.alt: Role-Based GenAI Training Curricula -- four role categories and four curriculum levels

content.text: The curriculum framework defines four levels, each targeting a different depth of engagement with GenAI tools. Roles are assigned a mandatory level based on how they interact with AI outputs, not their seniority. An end user who runs AI-generated text through a validation checklist needs Practitioner training. A senior executive who reads AI-summarised reports but makes no direct tool decisions needs only Foundation.
Understand what AI is, what it cannot do, and what the responsible use policy requires
Execute the specific AI-assisted workflow safely, validate outputs, escalate errors
Design better prompts, review AI quality, handle edge cases, coach others
Build, evaluate, and govern AI systems; red-team; design evals; implement LLMOps

Each curriculum module has prerequisites. Foundation modules must precede Practitioner modules; tool-specific workflow training cannot precede responsible use policy training. The dependency structure also defines the order in which modules can be completed and enables the learning management system to gate enrolment automatically.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class RoleCategory(Enum):
    END_USER = "end_user"
    KNOWLEDGE_WORKER = "knowledge_worker"
    TEAM_LEAD = "team_lead"
    DEVELOPER = "developer"


class CurriculumLevel(Enum):
    FOUNDATION = "foundation"
    PRACTITIONER = "practitioner"
    ADVANCED = "advanced"
    EXPERT = "expert"


@dataclass
class CurriculumModule:
    module_id: str
    title: str
    level: CurriculumLevel
    duration_hours: float
    learning_outcomes: list[str]
    assessment_method: str    # "quiz", "practical", "simulation", "portfolio"
    prerequisite_ids: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def can_enrol(self, completed_ids: set[str]) -> bool:
        return all(p in completed_ids for p in self.prerequisite_ids)


@dataclass
class RoleCurriculum:
    role_category: RoleCategory
    role_examples: list[str]
    required_modules: list[CurriculumModule]
    optional_modules: list[CurriculumModule] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def total_required_hours(self) -> float:
        return sum(m.duration_hours for m in self.required_modules)

    def completion_path(self) -> list[CurriculumModule]:
        """Return modules in dependency-safe order."""
        completed: set[str] = set()
        path: list[CurriculumModule] = []
        remaining = list(self.required_modules)
        while remaining:
            ready = [m for m in remaining if m.can_enrol(completed)]
            if not ready:
                raise ValueError("Circular dependency in curriculum")
            for m in ready:
                path.append(m)
                completed.add(m.module_id)
                remaining.remove(m)
        return path

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        issues = []
        if self.total_required_hours < 2:
            issues.append(f"Curriculum too short ({self.total_required_hours}h) -- minimum 2h for meaningful learning")
        assessment_types = {m.assessment_method for m in self.required_modules}
        if "practical" not in assessment_types and "simulation" not in assessment_types:
            issues.append("No practical or simulation assessment -- declarative knowledge only")
        return issues


# Example: Knowledge Worker curriculum (legal analyst)
legal_analyst_curriculum = RoleCurriculum(
    role_category=RoleCategory.KNOWLEDGE_WORKER,
    role_examples=["Legal Analyst", "Contracts Manager", "In-house Counsel"],
    required_modules=[
        CurriculumModule(
            module_id="F1",
            title="AI Concepts for Legal Professionals",
            level=CurriculumLevel.FOUNDATION,
            duration_hours=1.5,
            learning_outcomes=[
                "Explain how large language models generate output (next-token prediction)",
                "Describe what hallucination is and why it occurs",
                "Identify the difference between AI-assisted and AI-autonomous decisions",
            ],
            assessment_method="quiz",
        ),
        CurriculumModule(
            module_id="F2",
            title="Responsible Use Policy and Data Handling",
            level=CurriculumLevel.FOUNDATION,
            duration_hours=1.0,
            learning_outcomes=[
                "Identify which document types may not be submitted to the AI tool",
                "Apply the client data classification policy to AI inputs",
                "State who to contact when uncertain about permissible use",
            ],
            assessment_method="quiz",
            prerequisite_ids=["F1"],
        ),
        CurriculumModule(
            module_id="P1",
            title="Using ContractSummariser: Workflow and Validation",
            level=CurriculumLevel.PRACTITIONER,
            duration_hours=2.0,
            learning_outcomes=[
                "Execute the five-step extraction workflow for standard contracts",
                "Apply the validation checklist to verify liability figures, dates, and party names",
                "Identify the three known limitation categories for this tool",
                "Submit an AI error report via the incident channel",
            ],
            assessment_method="simulation",
            prerequisite_ids=["F1", "F2"],
        ),
        CurriculumModule(
            module_id="P2",
            title="Hallucination Detection for Legal Outputs",
            level=CurriculumLevel.PRACTITIONER,
            duration_hours=1.5,
            learning_outcomes=[
                "Recognise the surface patterns that correlate with hallucination risk",
                "Apply source-tracing to verify extracted figures against source document",
                "Distinguish between confident-sounding correct output and confident-sounding incorrect output",
            ],
            assessment_method="practical",
            prerequisite_ids=["P1"],
        ),
    ],
)

print(f"Role: {legal_analyst_curriculum.role_category.value}")
print(f"Total required hours: {legal_analyst_curriculum.total_required_hours}h")
print()
print("Completion path:")
for i, m in enumerate(legal_analyst_curriculum.completion_path(), 1):
    print(f"  {i}. [{m.level.value:12}] {m.title} ({m.duration_hours}h) -- assess: {m.assessment_method}")
print()
issues = legal_analyst_curriculum.validate()
if issues:
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"  WARNING: {issue}")
else:
    print("Curriculum validation passed")
Learner Progress Tracking and Cohort Readiness

The training programme must be measured against a go-live readiness gate, not just completion rates. The gate question is not 'how many people have started training?' but 'how many people are certified to use the tool safely?' A cohort that is 80% complete is not go-live ready if the uncompleted 20% are the roles with the highest daily tool exposure.

Foundation modules must be completed by all employees in scope, including those who will not use AI tools directly. The rationale: even employees who do not use AI tools will receive AI-generated outputs from colleagues, will be affected by organisational decisions made using AI, and will need to understand the responsible use policy that governs their organisation.
What AI can and cannot do.
Teach the mechanism plainly: next-token prediction, training data, temperature, why hallucination happens. Not marketing language about AI capabilities.
Make explicit that AI does not make decisions -- people do. The employee who uses AI output and does not validate it is accountable for the output, not the AI.
What categories of data may not be submitted to AI tools. What constitutes a permissible use. Who to contact with questions.
What to do when the AI is wrong.
The escalation path. The incident reporting channel. The reassurance that reporting errors is expected and non-punitive.

Practitioner modules are workflow-specific and tool-specific. Generic 'how to use AI' training at the Practitioner level is not sufficient. Employees need to learn the exact workflow their role follows, using the exact tool they will use, with the exact validation checklist they will apply. Generic training transfers less well and produces higher error rates in the first weeks of adoption.
Not 'upload the document and review the output' but the exact five-step process, with screenshots or screen recordings of the production tool.
The failure modes this specific tool has in this specific use case. Employees must be able to recognise when they are in a known-risk scenario.
The exact checks to perform on the AI output before using or delivering it. This checklist must be role-specific and output-specific.

A quiz cannot assess whether an employee can correctly validate an AI output. The assessment must include a practical exercise with realistic outputs, including at least one hallucination or error to detect.
Assessment Design: Why Quizzes Are Insufficient for Practitioner Level

Knowledge-based quizzes test recall. They do not test whether an employee can recognise a confident-sounding hallucination in a document they reviewed themselves, or whether they apply the validation checklist under time pressure. The gap between declarative knowledge ('I know I should validate the liability figure') and procedural skill ('I notice the liability figure looks inconsistent and check it against source') is what simulation-based assessment measures.
Does not test application under real conditions
Ability to execute workflow steps on realistic inputs
Requires pre-built exercise materials with known errors
Ability to detect errors and make correct decisions in realistic scenario
Higher design cost; requires realistic scenario authoring
Sustained performance quality across multiple tasks
Long time horizon; requires manager involvement
Ability to build, evaluate, and govern AI components
High investment; requires technical assessment rubric

A curriculum authored at programme launch becomes stale as the tool evolves, as new failure modes are discovered, and as the organisation's policies are updated. Curriculum maintenance must be scheduled, not reactive. Three triggers require curriculum review:
A model update or tool version change that alters output behaviour or failure modes -- the known limitations section and validation checklist must be updated before the new version reaches users.
A P1 or P2 incident that reveals a failure mode not covered in existing training -- the failure mode must be added to the Practitioner module before the next cohort begins.
A policy change (data handling, responsible use, accountability model) -- Foundation modules must be updated and re-taken by all employees in scope, not just new joiners.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class CurriculumVersion:
    version_id: str
    effective_date: date
    change_summary: str
    modules_updated: list[str]
    requires_recertification: bool    # must existing-certified employees retake?
    recertification_scope: str        # "all", "practitioner-and-above", "affected-roles"
    authored_by: str

    def recertification_notice(self) -> str:
        if not self.requires_recertification:
            return "No recertification required. New joiners use this version from effective date."
        return (
            f"Recertification required for: {self.recertification_scope}\n"
            f"Modules to retake: {', '.join(self.modules_updated)}\n"
            f"Complete by: {self.effective_date}"
        )


# Triggered by a P2 incident -- hallucination in nested liability structures
incident_triggered_update = CurriculumVersion(
    version_id="legal-analyst-v2",
    effective_date=date(2026, 8, 1),
    change_summary=(
        "P2 incident INC-2026-112: added nested liability structure as a known high-risk "
        "extraction scenario. Updated validation checklist step 3 to include cross-reference "
        "check for all liability figures in contracts with nested schedules."
    ),
    modules_updated=["P1", "P2"],
    requires_recertification=True,
    recertification_scope="all certified legal analysts",
    authored_by="A. Okonkwo + Legal Practice Director",
)

print(f"Curriculum update: {incident_triggered_update.version_id}")
print(f"Effective: {incident_triggered_update.effective_date}")
print(f"Changed: {incident_triggered_update.change_summary[:80]}...")
print()
print(incident_triggered_update.recertification_notice())
One universal training programme for all roles
End users learn more than they need; tool users learn less than they need
Map roles to levels before designing any content
Employees pass but cannot detect hallucinations under real conditions
Replace or supplement quiz with simulation exercise containing planted errors
Employees encounter edge cases with no framework for recognising them
Add a dedicated limitations module using real failure examples from the eval suite
Next cohort learns outdated failure mode guidance
Incident closure checklist includes mandatory curriculum review step
Completion rate measured without readiness gate
Go-live proceeds with uncertified users in critical roles
Define a minimum readiness threshold per role before go-live approval

Role-based GenAI training curricula assign different depths, content, and assessment methods based on how each role interacts with AI outputs in practice. Four levels -- Foundation (all employees), Practitioner (direct tool users), Advanced (power users and leads), and Expert (developers and AI engineers) -- each serve a distinct learning objective. Foundation modules teach AI concepts, the accountability model, and the responsible use policy. Practitioner modules are workflow-specific and tool-specific, assessed through simulation exercises that include planted errors to detect. Curriculum modules have explicit prerequisites that enforce learning sequence. Learner progress is tracked against a go-live readiness gate, not just a completion count. Curricula must be versioned and maintained: a P1 or P2 incident, a tool update, or a policy change each triggers a mandatory curriculum review before the next cohort begins, with recertification required for existing users when the failure mode or workflow changes.
